opus.cited_a: Expenses tied to holding restaurant premises rose.
opus.cited_b: Lease and property charges for our locations increased.
opus.reason: OBJECT is not the same. Side A "occupancy_costs" — "Expenses tied to holding restaurant premises rose." — is the broad umbrella category covering ALL costs of holding premises (rent, property taxes, insurance, CAM, maintenance). Side B "rent_and_related_expenses" — "Lease and property charges for our locations increased." — is the lease/rent-specific component of that umbrella. This is a component-vs-aggregate relationship: rent is one of the drivers that rolls UP into occupancy costs, so they sit at different granularities and are not the same underlying metric. MECHANISM also differs — occupancy costs can rise from property taxes or insurance without rent moving at all, so Side B is a downstream/subset driver of Side A rather than an identical cause. The instructions require the OBJECT to be clearly the same and exclude downstream/component relationships; here it is unclear-to-false. Default-to-DIFFERENT applies, and over-merging an aggregate with one of its components is exactly the permanent damage to avoid.

sonnet_a.cited_a: Expenses tied to holding restaurant premises rose.
sonnet_a.cited_b: Lease and property charges for our locations increased.
sonnet_a.reason: OBJECT unclear/not-same: "occupancy_costs" is typically a broader bucket (rent + property tax + insurance + utilities + CAM + depreciation on leasehold improvements) than "rent_and_related_expenses," which A's own quote doesn't narrow down ("expenses tied to holding premises" could include non-lease items) while B's quote is specifically "lease and property charges." SCOPE is unclear on both sides -- neither quote states company-owned vs. franchised, or segment vs. consolidated. MECHANISM is unclear on both sides -- neither quote gives the actual cause (new store growth, renegotiated leases, rate escalators, tax reassessment, etc.), just that a cost category "rose"/"increased." Since object equivalence is not established and scope/mechanism are both unclear, default to DIFFERENT per the rule.

sonnet_b.cited_a: Expenses tied to holding restaurant premises rose.
sonnet_b.cited_b: Lease and property charges for our locations increased.
sonnet_b.reason: OBJECT is not compellingly the same: "occupancy_costs" (A) reads as a broad bucket ("expenses tied to holding premises") that can include rent, property tax, insurance, CAM, utilities, and depreciation, while "rent_and_related_expenses" (B) names a narrower object ("lease and property charges") centered on lease payments. Neither quote confirms these resolve to the identical cost set. SCOPE is also unclear for both sides -- neither quote nor slice_tokens indicates whether these costs apply to company-owned vs. franchised locations, or a segment vs. consolidated population -- so same-scope cannot be confirmed. Since OBJECT is unclear and SCOPE is unclear, the rule requires DIFFERENT even though MECHANISM (rising real-estate-related costs) looks plausibly aligned.

opus.cited_b: Purchases driven by broken components increased.
opus.reason: OBJECT differs: Side A is a product-category driver ("durable components like brakes and pumps") — hard parts classified by part type; Side B is a demand-trigger driver ("broken components") — sales classified by why the purchase happened (failure/breakage). "Durable" and "broken" are near-opposite framings, not the same underlying thing. MECHANISM differs: A is general demand growth for a category of parts, which can arise from many causes (vehicle age, DIY, new builds); B is specifically failure/breakage-driven replacement. These overlap only by correlation (some hard parts are bought because they broke), not identity — many durable-component sales are not failure-driven and many failure-driven purchases are not durable/hard parts. With OBJECT and MECHANISM both unequal, the three-part test fails, so default to DIFFERENT.

sonnet_a.cited_b: Purchases driven by broken components increased.

Side A's object is a product category — "durable components" (hard parts like brakes/pumps) — with an unspecified cause of demand growth (could be more miles driven, fleet aging, new-vehicle builds, scheduled maintenance, etc.). Side B's object is purchases specifically attributed to a failure event — "driven by broken components" — which is a demand-reason classification that could apply to any part type, not just hard parts, and says nothing about the hard-parts category as such. Neither quote states that the hard-parts demand growth in A is CAUSED BY breakage as in B, so the causal mechanism is not shown to be the same, only possibly correlated. Scope (company-owned vs franchised, segment vs consolidated) is also unstated on both sides, so it can't be confirmed identical. Since not all three criteria are clearly satisfied by verbatim evidence, default to DIFFERENT.

Mechanism differs and is unclear for A: A only states demand "grew" with no stated cause (could be fleet aging, more miles driven, new-car attach, etc.), whereas B explicitly attributes the increase to breakage/failure. Since A never ties its growth to failure, sameness of mechanism is not established — and scope is unstated on both sides (no slice_tokens, no ownership/population given). With object unclear and mechanism unclear, at least two of the three required conditions fail, so per the default-to-DIFFERENT rule this is DIFFERENT.

opus.cited_a: The amount a typical party spends per visit rose on menu pricing.
opus.cited_b: Average ticket moved higher, with pricing more than offsetting lighter add-on attach.
opus.reason: OBJECT (same): Both name the average spend per restaurant transaction — Side A "The amount a typical party spends per visit," Side B "Average ticket moved higher." In restaurants "average check" and "average ticket" are standard synonyms, and a party-per-visit equals one ticket/check. SCOPE (same): Both are restaurant-level with no slice tokens and no ownership/segment restriction (neither says company-owned vs franchised or segment vs consolidated), so the transaction population is symmetric. MECHANISM (same): Both increases are driven by menu pricing — A "rose on menu pricing," B "pricing more than offsetting lighter add-on attach." B only adds that add-on attach softened while pricing dominated, so pricing is the shared primary causal driver, not a downstream effect. All three tests pass, compelling SAME over the DIFFERENT default.

sonnet_a.cited_a: The amount a typical party spends per visit rose on menu pricing.
sonnet_a.cited_b: Average ticket moved higher, with pricing more than offsetting lighter add-on attach.
sonnet_a.reason: Mechanism differs. Side A attributes the rise to a single cause: menu pricing, full stop. Side B describes a compound mechanism: pricing pushed the metric up while a separate, offsetting force -- lighter add-on attach (fewer add-ons per visit, a mix/volume effect) -- pushed it down, with pricing merely winning the net. That negative attach-rate headwind has no counterpart anywhere in Side A's quote. Treating these as the same driver would erase the fact that B's increase happened despite a weakening mix trend that A shows no sign of. Since the causal story is not identical (same MECHANISM fails), the pair defaults to DIFFERENT regardless of whether 'average check' and 'average ticket' name the same OBJECT.

sonnet_b.cited_a: The amount a typical party spends per visit rose on menu pricing.
sonnet_b.cited_b: Average ticket moved higher, with pricing more than offsetting lighter add-on attach.
sonnet_b.reason: Object is plausibly shared (average check/ticket are common restaurant-industry synonyms for per-visit spend), but mechanism and scope are not verifiably shared. Side A attributes the rise to a single factor, pure menu pricing ("rose on menu pricing"), with no mention of any offsetting effect. Side B describes a compound mechanism: pricing gains that "more than offset" a decline in add-on attach (mix/upsell rate) — i.e., two opposing causal forces netting to a smaller increase than pricing alone would produce. Since Side A gives no indication a mix/attach headwind was also present, and Side B gives no indication pricing was the sole driver, the two are not confirmed to describe the same causal story (simple pricing pass-through vs. pricing net of mix erosion). Additionally, neither quote specifies business population or ownership class (company-owned vs. franchised, segment vs. consolidated), so same-scope cannot be confirmed by quote either. Per the default-to-DIFFERENT rule, unclear/unconfirmed mechanism and scope mean this pair does not qualify as SAME.
